subject: ap-world-history
theme: Consequences of Industrialization, 1750–1900: Industrial capitalism drives European imperial expansion, creating a global system of colonial extraction and generating diverse forms of resistance.

core_concepts.title: New Imperialism: Causes and Mechanisms
core_concepts.summary: The 'new imperialism' of the 1870s–1914 differed from earlier European colonialism in its scale, speed, and ideological justification. Industrial capitalism created demand for raw materials (rubber, cotton, tin, copper) and markets for manufactured goods; industrialists lobbied governments for formal colonial control. Technological advantages — machine guns (Maxim gun), quinine (malaria prevention), steam-powered gunboats, telegraph communications — gave European powers overwhelming military superiority. Ideological justifications included Social Darwinism (survival of the fittest among nations), the 'civilizing mission,' and Rudyard Kipling's 'White Man's Burden' (1899).

core_concepts.title: The Scramble for Africa and the Berlin Conference
core_concepts.summary: Between 1880 and 1914, European powers partitioned sub-Saharan Africa from 10% to 90% under European control. The Berlin Conference (1884–85), convened by Bismarck, established rules for European partition of Africa: any European power could claim territory by establishing 'effective occupation.' African rulers and peoples were not invited. The Congo Free State was personally assigned to King Leopold II of Belgium, who ran it as a brutal rubber extraction enterprise, killing or mutilating millions — exposed by journalist E.D. Morel and activist Roger Casement, prompting international condemnation.

core_concepts.title: Colonial Economic Systems and Their Effects
core_concepts.summary: Colonial economies were organized to benefit the imperial power, not local populations. Plantation agriculture (rubber, cotton, sugar, tobacco) replaced subsistence farming. Cash-crop cultivation forced farmers to grow export crops rather than food, creating vulnerability to famine. Drain of wealth theory (Dadabhai Naoroji): India's economic surplus was systematically extracted to Britain through colonial taxation, trade imbalances, and salaries of British officials paid by Indian taxpayers. Railway networks built for resource extraction, not internal development. Indentured labor — workers contracted under near-slavery conditions — replaced enslaved labor after abolition on Caribbean and Pacific plantations.

core_concepts.title: Resistance to Imperialism
core_concepts.summary: Colonized peoples resisted in diverse ways. Armed resistance: Ethiopian Emperor Menelik II defeated Italy at the Battle of Adwa (1896), the only significant African military victory over a European colonial power. Zulu Wars (1879), Herero and Nama genocide (1904–08, German Southwest Africa). The Sepoy Mutiny/Indian Rebellion of 1857 — triggered by British violations of Hindu and Muslim religious practices — was the largest anti-British uprising before Indian independence. The Boxer Rebellion (China, 1900) was a popular uprising targeting foreign missionaries and commercial interests. Intellectual resistance: the Indian National Congress (1885) began demanding political reform.

core_concepts.title: East Asia: Unequal Treaties and Self-Strengthening
core_concepts.summary: China and Japan responded differently to Western pressure. China: the Opium Wars (1839–42, 1856–60) forced China to accept humiliating 'unequal treaties' granting Britain extraterritoriality, treaty ports, and a monopoly on opium sales. The Self-Strengthening Movement (1861–1895) attempted modernization while maintaining Confucian culture ('Chinese learning as substance, Western learning for function'), but failed — China was defeated in the Sino-Japanese War (1895). Japan: the Meiji Restoration (1868) succeeded by wholesale adoption of Western-style institutions, industrialization, and military organization, enabling Japan to defeat Russia (1905) and colonize Korea (1910).

core_concepts.title: Social Darwinism and Racism as Imperial Ideology
core_concepts.summary: Social Darwinism applied evolutionary theory to human societies, arguing that more powerful civilizations proved their superiority by dominating 'weaker' ones — giving pseudoscientific justification to racial hierarchy. Racism constructed categories of civilizational advancement, placing European (especially Anglo-Saxon) peoples at the apex and African and Asian peoples at the bottom, justifying colonialism as 'natural' and even beneficial. Scientific racism (phrenology, eugenics) claimed to provide empirical evidence for racial hierarchy. These ideologies were contested by colonized intellectuals who argued for equal humanity and cultural dignity.

key_terms.definition: Accelerated European colonial expansion in the 1870s–1914, driven by industrial capitalism's demand for raw materials and markets, and enabled by military technological superiority.
Meeting of European powers that established rules for partitioning Africa among themselves, requiring 'effective occupation' to validate territorial claims; Africans were not invited.
Rapid European colonization of sub-Saharan Africa, moving from 10% to 90% European control between 1880 and 1914.
Territory in Central Africa personally owned by King Leopold II of Belgium (1885–1908), where rubber extraction was enforced through mutilation and mass killing, killing millions.
Misapplication of Darwin's evolutionary theory to human societies, arguing that powerful nations' dominance proved their racial or civilizational superiority — used to justify imperialism.
Poem by Rudyard Kipling (1899) urging Americans to colonize the Philippines, presenting colonialism as a duty to 'civilize' non-European peoples — exemplifies imperial ideology.
Argument by Dadabhai Naoroji that British colonialism systematically extracted India's economic surplus through taxes, unequal trade, and British salaries paid by Indian taxpayers.
Contract labor system that replaced enslaved labor after abolition; workers (often from India or China) were bound to work for 5–7 years under near-slavery conditions on colonial plantations.
Ethiopian military victory over Italy under Emperor Menelik II — the only significant African defeat of a European colonial army — preserving Ethiopian independence.
Large-scale Indian rebellion against British East India Company rule, triggered by religious grievances among Indian soldiers (sepoys); led to British Crown assuming direct rule of India.
Chinese popular uprising targeting foreign missionaries and businesses; suppressed by an eight-nation military coalition, resulting in humiliating indemnity payments by China.
Treaties imposed on China and other Asian nations by Western powers after military defeats, granting extraterritoriality, low tariffs, and treaty ports — symbolizing Western imperial dominance.
Treaty provision exempting foreigners from the laws of the host nation, placing them under the jurisdiction of their home country's courts — resented as violation of sovereignty.
Chinese reform movement (1861–1895) attempting to modernize military technology while preserving Confucian culture; failed to prevent Chinese defeat in the Sino-Japanese War.

Political organization founded by educated Indians initially seeking greater participation in British Indian governance; became the primary vehicle for Indian independence under Gandhi in the 20th century.

exam_tip: Units 6 and 7-8 are deeply connected — imperialism's colonial humiliations directly motivated 20th-century independence movements. For causation essays, practice arguing: European industrialization → need for raw materials and markets → new imperialism → Scramble for Africa → African resistance → 20th-century nationalism. Know the contrast between Ethiopia (successfully resisted) and most of Africa (colonized) — examiners love asking why Ethiopia succeeded. On Social Darwinism, always explain it as ideology justifying economic extraction, not just racial prejudice — the economic motive is essential for full credit.
